title: Hollywood Is at War with Itself. Plus: The Inaugural “You Blew It!” Movie Awards.
format: Sean and Amanda work through the Warner Bros. HBO Max fallout and Disney's firehose of streaming announcements, then hand out the first “You Blew It!” awards for takes they got wrong.

segments.heading: Hollywood Is at War with Itself
segments.note: the Warner Bros. HBO Max fallout and Disney's investor-day deluge

films.title: Tenet
films.note: The bomb that, per Sean and Amanda, helped trigger the whole HBO Max decision. Nolan's public statements dominate the fight; they're rewatching it with Chris Ryan later in the week, this time with closed captions.

films.title: Wonder Woman 1984
films.note: The first Warner title to go day-and-date on HBO Max. Sean notes Warner reportedly paid Patty Jenkins and Gal Gadot upwards of $10M each to cover the back end, and Jenkins promptly signed on for a Star Wars movie.

films.title: Dune
films.note: Villeneuve's Variety op-ed decried the move; Amanda found it got closer to mourning the theatrical experience than Nolan did, while noting the back-end math clearly stung.

films.title: Soul
films.note: Cited among the Pixar titles going straight to Disney+ on Christmas rather than theaters.

films.title: Raya and the Last Dragon
films.note: Announced for Disney+ Premier Access, the paid-tier release model Disney used for Mulan.

films.title: Lightyear
films.note: The Buzz origin story. Chris Evans's tweet clarifying this is 'the human Buzz Lightyear that the toy is based on' was, per Sean, the single best thing to come out of the whole Disney presentation.

films.title: Toy Story
films.note: Sean was 13 when it came out; he credits it with keeping him on the hook for animated movies into adulthood, 25 years before he'd sit through a Disney investor call about its IP.

segments.heading: The 'You Blew It!' Awards: Reappraisals
segments.note: each host revisits a movie they were too hard on

films.title: Birds of Prey
films.note: Sean's mea culpa. He'd found Cathy Yan's film ugly and boring on first watch (sick, two days before the Oscars); on rewatch he came around to its aggression, its cast going for it, and Chad Stahelski's fight choreography.

films.title: Emma
films.note: Amanda's mea culpa. Autumn de Wilde's adaptation (Eleanor Catton screenplay, Anya Taylor-Joy, Josh O'Connor) lost her to Clueless comparisons in February; she now calls it delightful and blames pandemic-adjacent PVOD zoning-out.

segments.heading: The 'You Blew It!' Awards: How You Blew It
segments.note: each host names a film the other person got wrong

films.title: Miss Americana
films.note: Amanda's charge against Sean, a decade-long Taylor Swift skeptic. She reads Lana Wilson's doc as a selectively revealing, managed 'documercial' that still works; Sean holds firm that it's persona-management he finds distasteful.

films.title: I'm Thinking of Ending Things
films.note: Sean's charge against Amanda. He loved Charlie Kaufman's Ian Reid adaptation; Amanda stands by disliking it, arguing a cool idea isn't the same as an execution that lands emotionally.

segments.heading: The 'You Blew It!' Awards: The Filmmakers Blew It
segments.note: movies that should have worked on paper

films.title: The Last Thing He Wanted
films.note: Sean's pick: Dee Rees directing a Joan Didion adaptation with Anne Hathaway, Ben Affleck, Willem Dafoe. They turned to each other 10 minutes in at Sundance; it came and went with no conversation despite the pedigree.

films.title: Irresistible
films.note: Amanda's pick: Jon Stewart's political comedy with Steve Carell and Chris Cooper. In an election year it made no sense and wasn't funny; its politics seemed borrowed from 2006.
